skill.name: zentao-task-planner
skill.description: Plan ZenTao task schedules and run preview-first scripts for listing, creating, finishing, repairing, and closing ZenTao tasks.
skill.summary: Plan and review ZenTao task updates before executing them through local scripts.
skill.author: AriesYB

security_audit.summary: Static findings are partly confirmed: this skill intentionally runs local Python scripts, reads ZenTao credentials from environment files, and makes authenticated requests to a configured ZenTao server. I found no prompt injection attempt or evidence of malicious third-party exfiltration, but the credential handling and state-changing task operations require clear user review and warnings.

medium_findings.title: ZenTao Credentials Loaded and Sent to Configured Server
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill documents ZenTao account variables, loads .env files, reads ZENTAO_BASE_URL, ZENTAO_ACCOUNT, and ZENTAO_PASSWORD, and posts the account and password to the configured ZenTao login endpoint. This is expected for the skill, but it exposes sensitive credentials to local files and network transport controlled by configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential variables and login request are explicit in the documentation and implementation. The usage appears legitimate for ZenTao automation, but the data is clearly sensitive.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Scripts Can Mutate ZenTao Task State
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill includes scripts and client methods that can create tasks, finish tasks, repair effort dates, activate tasks, and close finished tasks. The documentation and command flags require preview or dry-run behavior before --execute, so this is not malicious, but accidental execution can alter external project records.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple command-line wrappers call authenticated client methods, and --execute switches preview behavior into real updates. The mutation paths are direct and confirmed by implementation context.

medium_findings.title: Local File Inputs and .env Storage Require Data Hygiene
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The scripts can read task plans, username mappings, JSON input files, and .env files from local paths. This is normal for a task-planning workflow, but users should avoid storing broad secrets or unreviewed project data in shared skill directories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-reading helpers and documented .env placement are explicit. The risk depends on local permissions and user handling, so the confidence is high but not absolute.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misidentified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Many external-command findings are fenced bash examples or inline script names in Markdown documentation. They describe how a user can run local Python scripts and do not contain Ruby backtick execution syntax.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown references or bash examples, not executable Ruby code. They still indicate the skill expects local script execution, which is handled separately as a medium risk.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Flags Are Scanner Noise
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The cited areas include task-type constants, UUID form fields, regex parsing, and ZenTao form field names rather than cryptographic algorithms or network scanning tools. I did not find evidence of hashing secrets, evasion, port scanning, or system reconnaissance intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines do not call cryptographic or scanning APIs. Confidence is slightly below very high because the static report aggregates many weak-pattern lines, but the representative flagged context is benign.

low_findings.title: High Entropy Markdown Findings Are Benign Content
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The high-entropy Markdown files contain Chinese instructions, TSV examples, command examples, and environment variable samples. I found no encoded payload or obfuscated executable content in the inspected references.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is readable documentation and examples. No encoded blob or hidden instruction was visible in the reviewed lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: dotenv Credential Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: The common helper loads .env files and reads ZenTao account credentials. This pattern is sensitive and should be documented clearly for users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dotenv loader and getenv calls are direct. The risk is credential exposure through local file handling, not hidden exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credentialed HTTP Session Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The client creates a requests session and posts account and password values to the configured ZenTao login endpoint. Users must trust the configured base URL and transport.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The request session and login parameters are explicit. The endpoint is user-configured, so the risk depends on configuration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Execute-Gated External State Changes
dangerous_patterns.description: Scripts default to preview or dry-run behavior but can create, finish, repair, and close tasks when --execute is provided.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The docs and wrapper code both show preview-first behavior with an execute flag. That control reduces risk but does not remove the external mutation hazard.

content.value_statement: Zentao planning and updates can require repeated copying, date checks, and manual task status changes. This skill turns task plans into reviewed ZenTao actions with preview-first scripts.
actual_capabilities: Plans task tables with names, requirement IDs, estimates, and start or end dates.
Finds current or next continuous workdays for schedule planning.
Lists ZenTao task views and can output summaries or TSV data.
Previews and optionally creates ZenTao tasks from a standardized TSV plan.
Previews and optionally finishes, repairs, or closes ZenTao tasks through authenticated scripts.
limitations: Requires local Python dependencies and valid ZenTao environment variables.
Requires access to a compatible ZenTao instance and account permissions.
State-changing operations must be reviewed before using the execute flag.
Task type parsing may fall back to built-in mappings if ZenTao pages cannot be parsed.

use_cases.title: Weekly Task Planning
use_cases.description: Create a reviewed task schedule with workday dates, estimates, and standardized task names.

use_cases.title: Task Creation Review
use_cases.description: Preview a TSV task plan before creating linked ZenTao tasks for multiple assignees.

use_cases.title: Task Status Maintenance
use_cases.description: Preview bulk completion, finish-date repair, or closure actions before updating ZenTao records.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Work Week
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan ZenTao tasks for my next work week. Ask for any missing name, requirement ID, dates, or task details before creating the table.

prompt_templates.title: Review Current Tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: List my active ZenTao tasks in summary form and identify which items need planning, completion, or closure review.

prompt_templates.title: Preview Task Creation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this confirmed task table to preview ZenTao task creation. Do not execute creation until I approve the preview.

prompt_templates.title: Audit and Repair Statuses
prompt_templates.prompt: Preview tasks in this date range that can be finished or repaired. Show the planned changes and wait for approval before any execute command.

output_examples.input: Plan next week tasks for one requirement and one developer.
output: A TSV-style task table with the required header.
Rows include assignee, requirement ID, task name, task type, estimate, start date, and end date.
The assistant asks for missing dates or requirement details before finalizing.

output_examples.input: Preview creation from a confirmed task plan.
output: A validation summary shows whether each task row can be created.
The assistant reports mapped ZenTao accounts, task types, estimates, and any blocking errors.
No task is created unless the user approves execution.

output_examples.input: Find tasks that can be closed after completion.
output: A preview lists finished but unclosed tasks with task names and counts.
The assistant asks for confirmation before closing records in ZenTao.

best_practices: Keep ZenTao credentials in a private local .env file with limited permissions.
Run preview commands first and compare every planned change with the source task list.
Use the execute flag only after the user confirms task IDs, assignees, dates, and estimates.
anti_patterns: Do not store production credentials in a shared repository or synced skill directory.
Do not execute bulk creation or completion from an unreviewed task table.
Do not assume dates, task owners, or requirement IDs when the user has not provided them.

faq.question: Does this skill change ZenTao data automatically?
faq.answer: No by default. The documented scripts preview actions first and require an execute flag for state-changing operations.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: It expects a ZenTao base URL, account, password, and name through environment variables or an env file.

faq.question: Can it create tasks for multiple people?
faq.answer: Yes. It can use a username mapping to convert names in a task table into ZenTao account IDs.

faq.question: Can it list existing tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. It can list active, assigned, finished, or closed task views and output summaries or TSV data.

faq.question: What should users review before execution?
faq.answer: Users should review task IDs, assignees, dates, estimates, task types, and the exact operation planned.

faq.question: Is this suitable for non-ZenTao project tools?
faq.answer: No. The scripts are specific to ZenTao pages, endpoints, and task fields.
